title: Roti

overview.description: Thin, unleavened Indian flatbreads made from atta and water, cooked on a hot tawa and optionally puffed directly over a flame.

method: Place the atta and salt, if using, in a large bowl.
Gradually add the warm water, mixing with your fingers until a soft dough forms.
Knead for 5–8 minutes until smooth and pliable.
Cover and rest for at least 20 minutes.
Divide the dough into 8 equal portions and roll each into a smooth ball.
Flatten one ball and dust lightly with atta.
Roll into a thin circle approximately 15–18cm in diameter.
Heat a dry tawa or heavy-based frying pan over medium-high heat until properly hot.
Place the roti on the hot pan.
Cook until the surface begins to show small bubbles and the underside develops pale brown spots.
Turn the roti and cook the second side until more brown spots appear.
Turn the roti once more and gently press around the edges and surface with a clean cloth or spatula to encourage it to puff.
Remove from the pan once cooked.
Keep the cooked roti wrapped in a clean tea towel.

learning.did-you-know: Roti are made throughout South Asia, with regional differences in flour, thickness, cooking method and terminology.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How a lean unleavened dough behaves differently from richer or softer flatbreads, and how direct heat can encourage a roti to puff.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A fundamental South Asian flatbread that is inexpensive, versatile and provides a useful opportunity to practise dough handling and high-heat cooking.
chapman-tips: Don't worry about perfect circles. Even experienced cooks took years to develop that speed and consistency.
The dough should be soft and pliable, not stiff.
Resting the dough makes rolling easier and improves the texture.
Use only a light dusting of flour when rolling.
The tawa needs to be hot enough to cook the roti quickly without burning it.
If using a gas flame, keep the roti moving and use tongs carefully.
For induction hobs, use a properly preheated tawa or heavy frying pan and cook the roti quickly. Pressing gently around the surface after turning can help encourage it to puff.
A roti does not have to puff to be successful; a soft, well-cooked roti is still a good roti.
notes.batch-cooking: Cooked roti freeze well. Cool completely, stack with baking paper between them if desired and freeze in a sealed bag or container. Reheat directly from frozen in a dry frying pan.
notes.leftovers: Use leftover roti as wraps, cut into strips for dipping or toast until crisp for a snack.
